name: 路德维希·范·贝多芬
brief: 贝多芬出生于波恩的一个音乐家庭，父亲酗酒且试图将他塑造为第二个莫扎特。1792年他前往维也纳，此后再未离开。约从1796年起听力开始衰退，到1814年已几乎全聋——他用谈话簿与人交流，却在完全失聪的状态下写出了《第九交响曲》和晚期弦乐四重奏等最伟大的作品。1827年去世时，两万多人参加了他的葬礼，维也纳万人空巷。
briefEn: Beethoven was born in Bonn into a musical family; his father was an alcoholic who tried to mold him into a second Mozart. In 1792 he moved to Vienna and never left. From around 1796 his hearing began to deteriorate, and by 1814 he was almost completely deaf — he communicated through conversation books, yet composed his greatest works, including the Ninth Symphony and the late string quartets, in total deafness. When he died in 1827, over 20,000 people attended his funeral, and the streets of Vienna came to a standstill.
contribution: 贝多芬是音乐史上最伟大的革命者。他将古典主义的形式推到了极限，并为浪漫主义打开了大门。《第三交响曲「英雄」》以其史无前例的规模和情感力量宣告了一个新时代的到来。他将交响曲从贵族沙龙的娱乐提升为人类精神的史诗，将钢琴奏鸣曲从客厅小品发展为哲学性的宏大叙事。他证明了音乐可以表达最深刻的人类思想。
contributionEn: Beethoven was the greatest revolutionary in the history of music. He pushed Classical forms to their absolute limits and flung open the door to Romanticism. The Third Symphony ('Eroica'), with its unprecedented scale and emotional power, heralded the dawn of a new era. He elevated the symphony from aristocratic entertainment to an epic of the human spirit, and transformed the piano sonata from a parlor piece into a grand philosophical narrative. He proved that music could express the most profound human ideas.
style: 贝多芬的创作通常被分为三个时期。早期（至约1802年）继承海顿与莫扎特传统，风格明朗有力；中期（1803-1814）被称为「英雄时期」，作品规模宏大、充满戏剧性冲突与英雄主义精神；晚期（1815-1827）进入全新的精神境界——音乐变得高度内省、结构自由、和声大胆超前，常常带有一种超越性的宁静与升华。
styleEn: Beethoven's output is traditionally divided into three periods. The early period (to c. 1802) carries on the Haydn–Mozart tradition with a bright, vigorous style. The middle period (1803–1814), known as the 'Heroic' phase, features works of vast scope filled with dramatic conflict and heroic spirit. The late period (1815–1827) enters an entirely new spiritual realm — the music becomes deeply introspective, structurally free, harmonically daring, and often suffused with a transcendent serenity and sublimity.

famousScores.name: 《第九交响曲「合唱」》 Op.125
famousScores.desc: 首次在交响曲中引入人声，「欢乐颂」成为欧盟盟歌
famousScores.descEn: The first symphony to incorporate vocal soloists and chorus; its 'Ode to Joy' became the anthem of the European Union
famousScores.detail:
《第九交响曲》Op.125创作于1822-1824年，是贝多芬在完全失聪状态下完成的最后一部交响曲，也是他的最后一部交响曲。这部作品以「席勒的欢乐颂」（An die Freude）为文本，在终乐章首次将人声（独唱与合唱）引入交响曲形式，彻底改变了交响曲的边界。全曲演奏时间约70分钟，规模之宏大在贝多芬之前的所有交响曲中都是前所未见的。

1824年5月7日维也纳首演时，贝多芬站在台上背对观众「指挥」（实则独立于真正指挥之外）。演出结束时，全场掌声雷动，但完全失聪的贝多芬浑然不觉，是身旁的独唱演员卡罗琳娜·昂格尔拉着他的衣袖，让他转过身来，才看见了眼前那片如潮的欢呼与泪水。这一幕成为音乐史上最令人动容的时刻之一。

终乐章的「欢乐颂」旋律以其朴素而宏大的力量成为人类博爱理想最动人的音乐表达。1985年，欧洲委员会选择这段旋律作为欧盟盟歌，它也是1989年柏林墙倒塌庆典上演奏的音乐，更在无数战争、灾难之后的纪念活动上响起。「所有人都成为兄弟」（Alle Menschen werden Brüder）——这句歌词和这段旋律已经超越了音乐的范畴，成为人类追求和平与共同尊严的永恒宣言。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 9, Op. 125, was composed between 1822 and 1824 in a state of total deafness — Beethoven's last symphony and one of the supreme achievements of Western music. For the first time in the history of the symphony, he introduced vocal soloists and chorus into the finale, setting Schiller's 'Ode to Joy' and shattering all previous conceptions of what a symphony could be. At about seventy minutes, its scale was unprecedented.

At the Vienna premiere on May 7, 1824, Beethoven stood on the stage with his back to the audience, 'conducting' alongside the real conductor. When the performance ended and the audience erupted, the totally deaf Beethoven had no idea of the ovation; the contralto soloist Caroline Unger had to turn him around to face the audience. He saw the sea of waving handkerchiefs and tearful faces. It is one of the most moving moments in music history.

The 'Ode to Joy' melody, with its simple grandeur, has become the most powerful musical expression of human fraternity ever conceived. In 1985 the European Council adopted it as the anthem of the European Union; it was played at the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989; it sounds at memorials after wars and disasters. 'Alle Menschen werden Brüder' — 'All men become brothers' — has transcended music to become humanity's most enduring musical declaration of the aspiration for peace and shared dignity.

famousScores.name: 《第五交响曲「命运」》 Op.67
famousScores.nameEn: Symphony No. 5 in C minor, Op. 67
famousScores.desc: 「命运叩门」的动机，可能是音乐史上最著名的四个音符
famousScores.descEn: Its 'fate knocking at the door' motif is perhaps the most famous four notes in all of music
famousScores.detail:
《第五交响曲》c小调Op.67创作于1804-1808年，1808年12月22日维也纳剧院首演。那晚是音乐史上最拥挤、最混乱、却也最重要的音乐会之一：同一场音乐会还首演了《第六交响曲「田园」》《第四钢琴协奏曲》和《合唱幻想曲》，演出在严冬中进行，剧院没有暖气，演出时间超过四小时，乐手中途罢演……尽管首演条件如此糟糕，这部交响曲却此后成为西方音乐史上最重要的单一作品之一。

开篇的「短短短长」动机（G-G-G-降E）被贝多芬的秘书和弟子辛德勒记录为贝多芬本人所说的「命运在叩门」。这四个音符以其惊人的简洁性成为整个交响曲发展的基因：它不仅出现在第一乐章，还以各种变形贯穿始终。第三乐章向第四乐章的过渡是贝多芬最伟大的结构发明之一——从c小调的黑暗神秘突然转入C大调的辉煌胜利，仿佛整个宇宙的光明在同一瞬间涌入。

第二次世界大战期间，「短短短长」的节奏（在摩尔斯电码中代表字母V，即「胜利」Victory）被盟军广播用作反法西斯的信号音，从而使贝多芬的这个动机在全球范围内成为了「胜利」的象征。这是古典音乐史上最具政治影响力的案例之一——一个18世纪的音乐动机成为了20世纪最重要战争的声音符号。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 5 in C minor, Op. 67, was composed between 1804 and 1808, premiering on December 22, 1808, in Vienna. That evening was one of the most chaotic yet historically consequential concerts ever given: the same program also premiered the Sixth Symphony ('Pastoral'), the Fourth Piano Concerto, and the Choral Fantasy, in a freezing theater without heat, lasting over four hours, with orchestra members threatening to stop. Despite these conditions, the Fifth would become the single most important orchestral work in Western music.

The opening 'short-short-short-long' motif (G–G–G–E-flat) was described by Beethoven's secretary Schindler as Beethoven saying 'Thus fate knocks at the door.' These four notes function as the genetic code of the entire symphony, appearing in transformed guises throughout all four movements. The transition from the third movement into the fourth — from C minor's dark mystery to C major's blazing triumph — is one of the most electrifying structural inventions in the history of music.

During World War II, the 'short-short-short-long' rhythm was recognized as the Morse code letter V for Victory, and the BBC adopted it as its wartime signal. This made Beethoven's motif the sonic symbol of the Allied struggle against fascism on a global scale — perhaps the most politically powerful application of a Classical musical idea in history.

famousScores.name: 《月光奏鸣曲》 Op.27 No.2
famousScores.desc: 第一乐章的三连音织体如月光洒落湖面
famousScores.descEn: The first movement's triplet figuration evokes moonlight shimmering across a lake
famousScores.detail:
《升c小调钢琴奏鸣曲》Op.27 No.2（「月光」）创作于1801年，献给贝多芬的学生朱利埃塔·圭恰尔迪，他曾对她有过一段不为人知的深情（她后来嫁给了另一位贵族）。「月光」这一别名并非贝多芬自己起的，而是诗人路德维希·莱尔斯塔卜在贝多芬死后发表的文章中，将第一乐章比作「月光照耀卢塞恩湖湖面」而得名，此后广为流传。

第一乐章（持续的柔板）是钢琴独奏文献中被演奏次数最多的乐章之一，其低音区稳定的八分音符脉动之上，中声部三连音涌动，高声部轻唱旋律，三层织体叠加形成一种梦境般的安静。贝多芬的学生车尔尼记载，贝多芬自己曾说这个乐章应该以「极薄的弱奏」演奏，如同「用手指触碰琴键」。然而许多钢琴家将第三乐章（急板激情）视为这首奏鸣曲真正的重心——那是贝多芬在钢琴上爆发出的一场情感风暴，极具挑战性。

「月光奏鸣曲」是全球最广为人知的古典钢琴曲之一，也是被改编最多的古典作品之一，出现在无数电影、广告、流行音乐改编、电子游戏和网络迷因中。但它的通俗声誉有时掩盖了其深刻的音乐内涵：贝多芬的副标题「准幻想曲奏鸣曲」（quasi una Fantasia）表明他自己意在打破传统奏鸣曲的快慢乐章顺序，将最慢的乐章置于首位，最快的置于末位，这在当时是一个大胆的形式实验。
famousScores.detailEn:
The Piano Sonata in C-sharp minor Op. 27 No. 2 'Moonlight' was composed in 1801 and dedicated to Beethoven's pupil Giulietta Guicciardi, for whom he had harbored an undisclosed deep affection (she later married another nobleman). The 'Moonlight' nickname was not Beethoven's own: the poet Ludwig Rellstab coined it after Beethoven's death, comparing the first movement to 'moonlight shimmering on the surface of Lake Lucerne,' and it has stuck ever since.

The first movement (Adagio sostenuto) is one of the most frequently played movements in all of piano literature: a steady bass pulse, rippling middle-register triplets, and a quietly singing melody in three interlocking layers create a dreamlike stillness. Beethoven reportedly told his pupil Czerny it should be played with 'the very thinnest pianissimo, as if touching the keys with the fingers' — yet many pianists consider the explosive, technically ferocious third movement (Presto agitato) the true emotional heart of the sonata.

The 'Moonlight' is one of the most universally recognized pieces of classical music worldwide and among the most arranged, appearing in films, advertisements, pop covers, video games, and internet memes beyond counting. Its popular fame can obscure its genuine musical boldness: Beethoven's own subtitle, 'quasi una Fantasia' (almost a fantasy), announced his deliberate subversion of the conventional fast-slow-fast sonata order, placing the slowest movement first and the fastest last — a bold formal experiment in 1801.

famousScores.name: 《第三交响曲「英雄」》 Op.55
famousScores.desc: 原献给拿破仑，后因其称帝而愤然撕去题献页
famousScores.descEn: Originally dedicated to Napoleon, then furiously stripped of its dedication when he crowned himself Emperor
famousScores.detail:
《第三交响曲》降E大调「英雄」Op.55创作于1803-1804年，是音乐史上最具革命性的单一作品之一。贝多芬将拿破仑视为革命理想的化身，最初题献为「波拿巴」。然而1804年拿破仑称帝的消息传来，据贝多芬的学生里斯记载，贝多芬勃然大怒，将题献页从谱面上撕下——「他也不过是一个普通人！」——最终以「为一位英雄而作」（Sinfonia eroica, composta per festeggiare il sovvenire di un grand Uomo）取代了原来的题献。

这部交响曲在多个维度上都开创了先河：演奏时间约50分钟，是当时所有交响曲中最长的一首；第一乐章的发展部以其惊人的长度和复杂性颠覆了古典奏鸣曲式的传统比例；第二乐章「葬礼进行曲」是西方音乐史上第一首在交响曲框架内以死亡为主题的宏篇乐章，直接影响了马勒等人的交响写作；终乐章以一段之前已在芭蕾《普罗米修斯的造物》中使用过的主题写成变奏，显示了贝多芬对古典形式的彻底自我更新。

指挥家卡洛斯·克莱伯认为「英雄」交响曲的第一乐章是「西方音乐中最伟大的单一乐章」。勃拉姆斯创作第一交响曲前犹豫了约20年，正是因为感到「英雄」的阴影太过庞大，难以逾越。这部交响曲标志着古典主义交响曲传统的终结和浪漫主义交响乐的诞生，对每一位此后的交响曲作曲家都产生了不可磨灭的影响。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 3 in E-flat major 'Eroica,' Op. 55, was composed in 1803–1804 and stands as one of the most revolutionary single works in music history. Beethoven had idolized Napoleon as the embodiment of revolutionary ideals, originally titling the score 'Bonaparte.' But when news arrived that Napoleon had crowned himself Emperor, Beethoven — according to his pupil Ries — flew into a rage, tore out the title page ('He is nothing but an ordinary man!'), and replaced the dedication with the phrase 'Composed to celebrate the memory of a great man.'

The symphony broke new ground in every dimension: at roughly fifty minutes, it was far the longest symphony ever written; the first movement's development section, with its extraordinary length and harmonic complexity, shattered the proportional conventions of Classical sonata form; the second-movement 'Funeral March' is the first truly death-centered movement in symphonic history, directly influencing Mahler and others; the finale's variations on a theme from Beethoven's own Prometheus ballet showed him completely reinventing inherited Classical forms from within.

Conductor Carlos Kleiber called the Eroica's first movement 'the greatest single movement in Western music.' Brahms hesitated for nearly twenty years before writing his First Symphony, feeling he could not escape the Eroica's overwhelming shadow. This symphony marks the end of the Classical symphonic tradition and the birth of the Romantic symphony — an influence on every subsequent symphonic composer that can never be overstated.

famousScores.name: 晚期弦乐四重奏 Op.127–135
famousScores.desc: 音乐史上最深邃的室内乐作品，至今仍令人望而生畏
famousScores.descEn: The most profound chamber music in existence, still awe-inspiring and formidable to this day
famousScores.detail:
晚期弦乐四重奏（Op.127、130、131、132、135，以及为Op.130写的「大赋格」Op.133）创作于1824-1826年，是贝多芬生命最后几年的精神遗嘱。全套作品在完全失聪的状态下创作，彼时贝多芬已基本与世隔绝，用谈话簿与人交流。这些四重奏突破了古典室内乐的所有惯例——曲式极度自由，Op.131一气呵成7个乐章不加一次完整中止，Op.132中间乐章标注着「来自一位病人向上帝致谢的感恩颂歌」，具有神秘主义般的超然宁静。

首演时，这套四重奏几乎令所有人迷惑——不只是当时的普通听众，甚至连最严肃的音乐家也感到困惑。「大赋格」Op.133在首演后被出版商要求从Op.130中单独出版，理由是它实在太过怪异和难以理解，作为终乐章会影响整个四重奏的接受度。舒伯特在临终前一月听到了Op.131，说：「在这之后，还有什么可以写的？」勃拉姆斯据说在演奏完Op.131后曾失声痛哭。

20世纪以后，这套晚期四重奏逐渐被认识为音乐史上最重要的单一系列作品之一。阿多诺在哲学著作中反复讨论它们作为「负面辩证法」的音乐体现；勋伯格从中发现了现代音乐的萌芽；巴托克将晚期四重奏作为自己弦乐四重奏写作的直接出发点。今天，演奏这套四重奏被视为弦乐四重奏演奏家生涯中最严峻的艺术挑战，任何一个四重奏组都必须用穷毕生之力去诠释。
famousScores.detailEn:
The late string quartets — Op. 127, 130, 131, 132, 135, and the stand-alone 'Grosse Fuge' Op. 133 — were composed between 1824 and 1826, the last years of Beethoven's life, in a state of total deafness and near-total social isolation. They violate every Classical chamber music convention: Op. 131 flows through seven connected movements without a single conclusive cadence between them; the middle movement of Op. 132 is inscribed 'Heiliger Dankgesang' (Holy Song of Thanksgiving from a convalescent to the Deity), sustaining a near-mystical otherworldly calm.

At their premieres, these quartets baffled almost everyone — not only ordinary listeners but the most serious musicians. The 'Grosse Fuge' was demanded by Beethoven's publisher as a separate publication because it was considered too strange to serve as a finale. Schubert heard Op. 131 a month before his own death and said: 'After this, what is left for us to write?' Brahms reportedly wept after playing through Op. 131.

From the 20th century onward, these quartets came to be recognized as the single most important series of works in music history. Adorno analyzed them repeatedly in his philosophy as the embodiment of 'negative dialectics'; Schoenberg found in them the seeds of modern music; Bartok took them as the direct point of departure for his own string quartet writing. Today, performing the cycle is regarded as the supreme artistic challenge of a string quartet's career — one that demands a lifetime to even begin to approach.

famousScores.name: 《第五钢琴协奏曲「皇帝」》 Op.73
famousScores.desc: 最辉煌的钢琴协奏曲，以帝王般的气势开创了浪漫主义协奏曲的先河
famousScores.descEn: The most magnificent piano concerto ever written — the archetype of the Romantic concerto
famousScores.detail:
《第五钢琴协奏曲》降E大调「皇帝」Op.73创作于1809-1810年，是贝多芬创作的最后一首钢琴协奏曲。1809年拿破仑军队炮轰维也纳，贝多芬在耳聋加剧的情况下躲在弟弟家的地窖里，用枕头捂着耳朵保护残存的听力，在炮声中写下了这部辉煌的协奏曲——这一对比（外部的战争破坏与内心创造力的巅峰）令人叹为观止。「皇帝」的绰号并非贝多芬所起，而是英国出版商或军官弗里德里希·卡尔克布伦纳据传在听到首演后脱口而出的赞叹。

这首协奏曲彻底改变了钢琴协奏曲的传统格局：独奏钢琴不再在管弦乐引子之后才出场，而是在第一个和弦之后立即以宏伟的华彩段落登场，宣告了独奏家与管弦乐队全新的平等乃至主导关系。第二乐章（柔板）的主题以弱音弦乐奏出一支圣咏般的旋律，随后钢琴以最轻柔的颤音和弦进入，这一静谧时刻被许多人认为是贝多芬最美丽的器乐页面之一。

「皇帝」协奏曲是此后一个半世纪浪漫主义钢琴协奏曲（从肖邦、李斯特、勃拉姆斯到拉赫玛尼诺夫）的直接原型。它确立了独奏家作为音乐诠释者和精神主角的地位——不再是宫廷演奏者，而是与管弦乐队在平等基础上展开对话的艺术家。卡内基音乐厅开幕后的首场演出（1891年）便以这首协奏曲的前辈「第四协奏曲」开场，显示了贝多芬协奏曲在音乐文化中无可撼动的经典地位。
famousScores.detailEn:
Piano Concerto No. 5 in E-flat major 'Emperor,' Op. 73, was composed in 1809–1810, Beethoven's last piano concerto. In the spring of 1809, Napoleon's army bombarded Vienna; Beethoven, his hearing already devastated, sheltered in his brother's cellar with pillows pressed over his ears to protect his remaining hearing — and in that destruction composed one of the most triumphant works in the orchestral repertoire. The nickname 'Emperor' was not Beethoven's own but is attributed to the English publisher or musician Friedrich Kalkbrenner, who reportedly exclaimed it after hearing the premiere.

This concerto overturned the traditional concerto format: instead of waiting for the orchestral introduction to conclude, the solo piano enters immediately after the opening chord with a blazing cadenza — announcing a new relationship of dominance and equality between soloist and orchestra. The slow movement (Adagio un poco mosso) presents a chorale-like theme in muted strings, then the piano enters in the softest trilled chords; this moment of luminous hush is considered by many listeners the most beautiful page Beethoven ever wrote for piano and orchestra.

The 'Emperor' became the direct archetype for the Romantic piano concerto across the next century and a half — from Chopin, Liszt, and Brahms to Rachmaninoff. It established the soloist as the spiritual protagonist of the concerto drama: no longer a court entertainer, but an equal partner — indeed a dominant voice — in dialogue with the orchestra. Its legacy is the entire tradition of the Romantic virtuoso piano concerto.

famousScores.name: 《小提琴协奏曲》D大调 Op.61
famousScores.nameEn: Violin Concerto in D major, Op. 61
famousScores.desc: 小提琴协奏曲文献中最崇高的作品，沉静中蕴含无限深邃
famousScores.descEn: The most sublime work in the violin concerto repertoire — infinite depth contained in serene outward calm
famousScores.detail:
《小提琴协奏曲》D大调Op.61创作于1806年，是贝多芬唯一一首小提琴协奏曲，也是整个小提琴协奏曲文献中公认的「王冠」。1806年12月23日首演于维也纳，由小提琴家弗兰茨·克莱门特（Clement，非克莱门蒂）担任独奏，据记载他在首演之前才第一次看到总谱，几乎是在视奏——贝多芬直到演出前夕才完成了总谱。这种特殊的创作仓促感或许也解释了为何第一乐章如此长篇大论，慢乐章却如此平静——仿佛一种刻意的留白。

这首协奏曲在首演后反响冷淡，此后几十年几乎无人演奏。直到1844年，年仅12岁的少年小提琴家约瑟夫·约阿希姆在门德尔松指挥下在伦敦首次演奏了这首作品，获得了巨大成功，从此将其永久确立为小提琴协奏曲文献的「女王」。约阿希姆此后将这首协奏曲列为他曲目的核心，一生演奏了无数次，无论演出多少次，他总在每次演出前夕重新研习，称它是「无法穷尽的」。

第一乐章以四下定音鼓声开场——这是小提琴协奏曲开头从未有过的写法——随后管弦乐平静地展开，带着一种冥想的宁静。小提琴进入时，如同从高处俯瞰大地的眼睛，凌驾于管弦乐织体之上却又融为一体。门德尔松、勃拉姆斯、西贝柳斯在各自创作小提琴协奏曲时，都以贝多芬的这首作品为终极参照，称它为「不可能超越的原型」。
famousScores.detailEn:
The Violin Concerto in D major, Op. 61, written in 1806, is the only violin concerto Beethoven ever composed — and the acknowledged crown of the entire violin concerto repertoire. At its premiere in Vienna on December 23, 1806, the soloist Franz Clement (the violinist, not the pianist Clementi) reportedly saw the score for the first time only at the performance — Beethoven had not finished the manuscript until the eve of the premiere. Perhaps this partly explains the first movement's unhurried expanse and the slow movement's almost contemplative stillness.

The concerto was received coolly at its premiere and lay largely unperformed for decades. In 1844, the twelve-year-old Joseph Joachim played it in London under Mendelssohn's baton and scored a triumph that permanently established it as the 'Queen' of violin concertos. Joachim made it the centerpiece of his concert repertoire for the rest of his long career, reportedly restudying it before every performance, calling it 'inexhaustible.'

The first movement opens with four quiet timpani strokes — unprecedented as an opening for a violin concerto — and the orchestra unfolds meditatively before the violin enters above the orchestral texture like an eye surveying the world from a great height. Mendelssohn, Brahms, and Sibelius each cited Beethoven's Violin Concerto as the ultimate reference point when composing their own — 'the archetype that cannot be surpassed.' It is the most inwardly serene work in Beethoven's entire middle-period output.

famousScores.name: 《升c小调弦乐四重奏》 Op.131
famousScores.nameEn: String Quartet in C-sharp minor, Op. 131
famousScores.desc: 贝多芬晚期四重奏中的极峰，七个乐章一气呵成
famousScores.descEn: The summit of Beethoven's late quartets — seven movements played without a break
famousScores.detail:
《升c小调弦乐四重奏》Op.131创作于1826年，是贝多芬晚期四重奏中篇幅最长、结构最特殊的一首，也被许多音乐家和学者认为是贝多芬所有作品中最伟大的一首。全曲七个乐章不加完整中止地连续演奏，从升c小调的赋格开场，途经D大调、A大调、E大调等各个调性区域，最终回到升c小调的疾风骤雨式终曲。贝多芬在结构上已经完全突破了传统的多乐章框架，七个乐章更像是一部单乐章的连续变奏式梦境。

舒伯特临终前一月听到这首四重奏的演奏，留下了那句令人心碎的话：「在这之后，还有什么可以写的？」贝多芬本人据说将这首四重奏视为他最满意的作品。第四乐章的行板变奏，以一个极简朴的主题展开六个风格迥异的变奏，情感涵盖了从天真无邪到深邃痛苦的全部人类情感，被指挥家卡洛斯·克莱伯称为「音乐中不可思议的时刻，无法用语言解释的美」。

20世纪以来，Op.131成为哲学家、心理学家和神学家讨论最多的音乐作品之一。阿多诺在其哲学巨著《美学理论》中多次援引这首四重奏作为「艺术在现代性中的处境」的象征；塔可夫斯基在1983年的电影《乡愁》中使用了这首四重奏的片段；国际宇航员马克·凯利在太空中演奏了Op.131第一乐章的弦乐改编，作为人类文明的使者。这首升c小调四重奏已经成为人类最深邃的精神探索的代名词之一。
famousScores.detailEn:
String Quartet in C-sharp minor, Op. 131, composed in 1826, is the longest and most unconventionally structured of Beethoven's late quartets — and the work that many musicians and scholars regard as the greatest of all his compositions. Its seven movements play without a complete break, opening with a slow fugue in C-sharp minor, moving through D major, A major, E major, and back to a storm-driven C-sharp minor finale. The structure entirely transcends the traditional multi-movement framework: the seven movements feel more like a single continuous variations-dream.

Schubert heard Op. 131 a month before his own death and said — heartbreakingly — 'After this, what is left for us to write?' Beethoven reportedly considered it his finest work. The fourth-movement Andante and variations presents a near-childlike simple theme followed by six variations of wildly contrasting emotional worlds — innocence, rapture, anguish, serenity — which conductor Carlos Kleiber called 'a moment in music that cannot be explained in words, only experienced.'

From the 20th century onward, Op. 131 became one of the most discussed musical works in philosophy, psychology, and theology. Adorno repeatedly cited it in his Aesthetic Theory as a symbol of 'the situation of art in modernity.' Tarkovsky used it in his 1983 film Nostalghia. Astronaut Mark Kelly performed an arrangement of its first movement in space as an ambassador of human civilization. The C-sharp minor Quartet has become synonymous with the deepest reaches of human spiritual inquiry.

famousScores.name: 《第32号钢琴奏鸣曲》c小调 Op.111
famousScores.nameEn: Piano Sonata No. 32 in C minor, Op. 111
famousScores.desc: 贝多芬最后一首钢琴奏鸣曲，以宇宙般的宁静告别了这一体裁
famousScores.descEn: Beethoven's last piano sonata — a cosmic farewell to the form, ending in transcendent stillness
famousScores.detail:
《第32号钢琴奏鸣曲》c小调Op.111创作于1821-1822年，是贝多芬32首钢琴奏鸣曲的终点。这首奏鸣曲仅有两个乐章——第一乐章（庄严的奏鸣快板）充满激烈的戏剧冲突，以c小调的阴暗力量呈现人间挣扎；第二乐章（柔板慢板、变奏曲）则以C大调的宁静升华，以一系列变奏将音乐逐渐带入近乎无声的超脱境界，最终以颤音和泛音的消逝结束。没有第三乐章——贝多芬就此告别了钢琴奏鸣曲，这一「沉默」本身即是一种宣言。

托马斯·曼的小说《浮士德博士》中有一段著名的讨论：钢琴教师克雷奇马尔向学生解释为何这首奏鸣曲只有两个乐章：「没有第三乐章……他放弃了它。」「为什么？」「因为他已经说完了……当他到达这里，对一个奏鸣曲来说，他已经超越了奏鸣曲……他进入了人类音乐所能达到的最后边界之外。」这段虚构的对话触及了这首奏鸣曲的本质——它不是未完成，而是恰恰在最适合的地方停止。

第二乐章中著名的「变奏曲」以c音（C大调主音）上方的连续颤音织体制造出一种仿佛超脱重力的漂浮感，贝多芬在这里发展出了一种后来被称为「颤音音乐」的新写法，20世纪的利盖蒂在他的钢琴练习曲中明确继承了这一传统。格伦·古尔德称Op.111第二乐章为「贝多芬最深刻的精神遗嘱」，阿尔弗雷德·布伦德尔则用一生的时间演奏和诠释它，称之为「我们所拥有的最接近神圣的音乐」。
famousScores.detailEn:
Piano Sonata No. 32 in C minor, Op. 111, composed in 1821–1822, is the last of Beethoven's thirty-two piano sonatas. It has only two movements: the first (Maestoso — Allegro con brio ed appassionato) is a drama of fierce C minor conflict and heroic struggle; the second (Arietta: Adagio molto semplice e cantabile) is a set of variations in C major that progressively elevates the music toward a state of near-silence and transcendence, ending in trills and overtones that simply dissolve into the air. There is no third movement — and that silence is itself a statement.

Thomas Mann's novel Doctor Faustus contains a celebrated passage in which the piano teacher Kretschmar explains the missing finale to his students: 'No third movement... he gave it up. Because he had come to the end of the sonata... He had gone beyond the sonata — into a realm that the sonata could no longer contain.' This fictional dialogue touches the essence of the work: it is not unfinished but ends precisely where it must.

The second movement's famous 'trillo' technique — sustained tremolando figuration that seems to suspend gravity — developed a new mode of keyboard writing that Ligeti explicitly carried forward in his piano études a century and a half later. Glenn Gould called Op. 111's second movement 'Beethoven's deepest spiritual testament.' Alfred Brendel devoted a lifetime of performances and writings to it, calling it 'the music closest to the divine that we possess.'

famousScores.name: 《庄严弥撒》 Op.123
famousScores.desc: 贝多芬最伟大的声乐作品，对神圣与人性的极度深沉探索
famousScores.descEn: Beethoven's greatest vocal work — a profoundly searching exploration of the sacred and the human
famousScores.detail:
《庄严弥撒》Op.123创作于1819-1823年，是贝多芬为鲁道夫大公就任奥洛穆茨大主教而写，但完成时已大大超过了典礼使用的范围和规模。贝多芬本人将其视为自己最伟大的作品——他在写给出版商的信中说：「这是我迄今最大的作品」。全曲演奏时间约80分钟，以独唱、合唱和管弦乐队共同构成，其规模与精神雄心可与同时代唯一能与之抗衡的海顿《b小调弥撒》（《纳尔逊弥撒》）和贝多芬自己的《第九交响曲》相媲美。

《庄严弥撒》最震撼人心的段落是「上主垂怜」（Kyrie）的赋格、「信经」中的「基督肉身降临」（Et incarnatus est）——贝多芬以双簧管、长笛和小提琴的轻柔旋律描写神圣降临的神秘，随后突然转入力量强劲的「十字架受难」段落——以及终章「和平颂」（Dona nobis pacem），在宁静的祈祷中突然插入军号和定音鼓的「战争」片段，表达了贝多芬对战争与和平的亲身体验（他亲历了拿破仑战争对维也纳的破坏）。

《庄严弥撒》在贝多芬生前只演出过一次完整版本（1824年，比《第九交响曲》还早）。勃拉姆斯深入研究了这部作品并将其列为「有史以来最伟大的合唱作品之一」，与巴赫的《b小调弥撒》并列。这部作品对19-20世纪宗教音乐的影响是深远的：勃拉姆斯的《德意志安魂曲》、威尔第的《安魂曲》乃至布鲁克纳的弥撒曲，都在精神上继承了贝多芬在这部作品中树立的崇高标准。
famousScores.detailEn:
Missa Solemnis, Op. 123, was composed between 1819 and 1823, originally intended for the installation of Archduke Rudolf as Archbishop of Olmütz — but it far outgrew any ceremonial purpose. Beethoven himself regarded it as his greatest work, writing to his publisher: 'I consider it the greatest work I have yet composed.' At some eighty minutes for soloists, chorus, and orchestra, its scale and spiritual ambition are matched in the choral repertoire only by Bach's B minor Mass and Beethoven's own Ninth Symphony.

The most overwhelming passages include the Kyrie fugue; the 'Et incarnatus est' in the Credo — where flute, oboe, and solo violin paint the mystery of divine incarnation in the most hushed tones before exploding into the Crucifixus — and the finale 'Dona nobis pacem,' a prayer for peace shattered by sudden fanfares and kettledrums representing war, expressing Beethoven's personal experience of Napoleon's devastation of Vienna.

The Missa Solemnis received only one complete performance in Beethoven's lifetime (1824, before the Ninth Symphony's premiere). Brahms studied it deeply and listed it alongside Bach's B minor Mass as 'one of the greatest choral works ever composed.' Its influence on 19th- and 20th-century sacred music is immense: Brahms's German Requiem, Verdi's Requiem, and Bruckner's masses all stand in spiritual lineage with the standard of sublimity Beethoven established here.

influence: 贝多芬的影响贯穿整个19世纪和20世纪。瓦格纳从他的交响曲中发展出了乐剧理念；勃拉姆斯在「英雄」的阴影下迟迟不敢动笔写交响曲；马勒将贝多芬的交响规模推向了极端。他的晚期作品超越了时代，在20世纪才真正被理解——序列主义者在其中发现了无调性的萌芽，简约主义者在其中找到了静默的力量。他从根本上改变了社会对音乐家角色的认知：音乐家不再是仆人，而是先知。
influenceEn: Beethoven's influence pervades the entire 19th and 20th centuries. Wagner developed his music-drama concept from Beethoven's symphonies; Brahms long hesitated to write a symphony under the shadow of the 'Eroica'; Mahler pushed Beethoven's symphonic scale to its extremes. His late works transcended their era and were only truly understood in the 20th century — serialists discovered the seeds of atonality in them, and minimalists found the power of silence. He fundamentally transformed society's perception of the musician: no longer a servant, but a prophet.
